questions.prompt: A bug appears in Safari on iPhone but you cannot reproduce it in Chrome on your laptop. What is the most likely cause?
options: The iPhone has a hardware defect
Safari and Chrome use different rendering engines, so they interpret the same CSS differently
The page was not deployed to the mobile server
questions.explanation: Safari uses WebKit and desktop Chrome uses Blink. Different engines can render the same CSS and features differently, producing browser-specific bugs.

questions.prompt: Your team cannot test all 200+ browser/OS/device combinations. What is the soundest way to choose which to test?
options: Test the combinations the developers personally own
Test only the newest browser versions because old ones are rare
Build a matrix from real user analytics and prioritize the combos your actual users use
Test every combination but only run a single smoke check on each
questions.explanation: A compatibility matrix should be driven by real usage data so testing effort lands on the browsers, OSes, and devices your users actually have.

questions.prompt: On a checkout page, a button is covered by the order summary only at phone width; on desktop it looks fine. What does this tell you?
options: It is a false alarm because desktop is correct
It is a backend bug in the order service
It is a real responsive layout bug that only surfaces below a certain viewport width
The button was deleted on mobile
questions.explanation: Layouts reflow at breakpoints. An overlap that appears only at narrow widths is a genuine responsive bug you would miss if you only tested desktop.

questions.prompt: Which set of viewport widths best represents mobile, tablet, and desktop for a first pass of responsive testing?
questions.explanation: Roughly 375px (mobile), 768px (tablet), and 1280px (desktop) span the breakpoints where responsive layouts typically change, giving broad coverage in three checks.
